The Knights were based in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), and played at the [Omni Coliseum](/wiki/Omni_Coliseum "Omni Coliseum"). In 1994 the Knights won the Turner Cup which was the IHL championship. The team became the [Quebec Rafales](/wiki/Quebec_Rafales "Quebec Rafales") in 1996.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Professional ice hockey team

| Atlanta Knights | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |

| League | [International Hockey League](/wiki/International_Hockey_League_(1945%E2%80%932001) "International Hockey League (1945–2001)") |
| Conference | Eastern |
| Division | Central |
| Founded | [1992](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_IHL_season "1992–93 IHL season") |
| Folded | [1996](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_IHL_season "1995–96 IHL season") |
| Home arena | [Omni Coliseum](/wiki/Omni_Coliseum "Omni Coliseum") |

| Owner | David Berkman[[1]](#cite_note-FWL-1) |

The **Atlanta Knights** were a [minor league](/wiki/Minor_league "Minor league") professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team in the [International Hockey League](/wiki/International_Hockey_League_(1945%E2%80%932001) "International Hockey League (1945–2001)") from 1992 to 1996. The Knights were based in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), and played at the [Omni Coliseum](/wiki/Omni_Coliseum "Omni Coliseum"). In 1994 the Knights won the Turner Cup which was the IHL championship. The team became the [Quebec Rafales](/wiki/Quebec_Rafales "Quebec Rafales") in 1996.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlanta_Knights&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Atlanta Knights was an IHL expansion team in 1992 as the top farm team of the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s [Tampa Bay Lightning](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Lightning "Tampa Bay Lightning"). They won the [Turner Cup](/wiki/Turner_Cup "Turner Cup") in the 1993–94 season. The Knights featured the first professional [black](/wiki/Black_people "Black people") head coach in [John Paris Jr.](/wiki/John_Paris_Jr. "John Paris Jr.") The Knights were also one of the first professional teams to play a female goalie, [Manon Rhéaume](/wiki/Manon_Rh%C3%A9aume "Manon Rhéaume"), in a regular-season game.

### Record in Atlanta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlanta_Knights&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Record in Atlanta")]

| Year | GP | W | L | RT | PTS | Finish | Playoffs |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1992–93 | 82 | 52 | 23 | 7 | 111 | 1st, Atlantic | Lost semi-finals |
| **1993–94** | **81** | **45** | **22** | **14** | **104** | **1st, Midwest** | **Won [Turner Cup](/wiki/Turner_Cup "Turner Cup")** |
| 1994–95 | 81 | 39 | 37 | 5 | 83 | 3rd, Central | Lost in Round One |
| 1995–96 | 82 | 32 | 41 | 9 | 73 | 4th, Central | Lost in Round One |

#### 1992–93: Knightmare on Peachtree Street

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlanta_Knights&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1992–93: Knightmare on Peachtree Street")]

Led by team captain & IHL legend [Jock Callander](/wiki/Jock_Callander "Jock Callander") and a 40-goal explosion from right winger [Keith Osborne](/wiki/Keith_Osborne "Keith Osborne") (each in their only season with the club), head coach [Gene Ubriaco](/wiki/Gene_Ubriaco "Gene Ubriaco")'s Knights would prove to be among the league's most formidable sides, amassing franchise single-season records in wins (52), points (111) & goals scored (333) en route to claiming the Atlantic Division championship.

The initial Knights roster featured many players that would go on to cement themselves into the folklore of the club, among them centers [Brent Gretzky](/wiki/Brent_Gretzky "Brent Gretzky") & [Colin Miller](/wiki/Colin_Miller_(ice_hockey,_born_1971) "Colin Miller (ice hockey, born 1971)"); wingers [Christian Campeau](/w/index.php?title=Christian_Campeau&action=edit&redlink=1 "Christian Campeau (page does not exist)"), [Stan Drulia](/wiki/Stan_Drulia "Stan Drulia") and [Jason Ruff](/wiki/Jason_Ruff "Jason Ruff"); defencemen [Jeff Buchanan](/wiki/Jeff_Buchanan "Jeff Buchanan"), [Eric Dubois](/wiki/Eric_Dubois "Eric Dubois"), [Chris LiPuma](/wiki/Chris_LiPuma "Chris LiPuma") and [Shawn Rivers](/wiki/Shawn_Rivers "Shawn Rivers"); and goalkeepers [Jean-Claude Bergeron](/wiki/Jean-Claude_Bergeron "Jean-Claude Bergeron") and [Mike Greenlay](/wiki/Mike_Greenlay "Mike Greenlay").

The inaugural Knights campaign also featured the North American professional hockey debuts of both [Manon Rhéaume](/wiki/Manon_Rh%C3%A9aume "Manon Rhéaume") (2 games, 0–1–0, 6.36 GAA) and the [1992 NHL entry draft](/wiki/1992_NHL_entry_draft "1992 NHL entry draft")'s #1 overall pick, [Roman Hamrlik](/wiki/Roman_Hamrlik "Roman Hamrlik") (2 games, one goal, one assist, two points, 2 penalty minutes), a future three-time NHL All-Star.

#### 1993–94: The Knightmare Continues...and Comes True – Turner Cup Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlanta_Knights&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1993–94: The Knightmare Continues...and Comes True – Turner Cup Champions")]

In 1993–94, the Knights became the first Atlanta-based sports franchise since the 1968 [Atlanta Chiefs](/wiki/Atlanta_Chiefs "Atlanta Chiefs") of the [North American Soccer League](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League_(1968%E2%80%931984) "North American Soccer League (1968–1984)") to win a professional championship, capturing the [Turner Cup](/wiki/Turner_Cup "Turner Cup") in a best-of-seven Finals victory over the [Fort Wayne Komets](/wiki/Fort_Wayne_Komets "Fort Wayne Komets"), four games to two.

The season-long goalkeepers [J.C. Bergeron](/w/index.php?title=J.C._Bergeron&action=edit&redlink=1 "J.C. Bergeron (page does not exist)") (27–11–7, 3.07) and [Mike Greenlay](/wiki/Mike_Greenlay "Mike Greenlay") (16–10–4, 3.58) were awarded the [James Norris Memorial Trophy (IHL)](/wiki/James_Norris_Memorial_Trophy_(IHL) "James Norris Memorial Trophy (IHL)"). The play from off-season acquisitions [Steve Larouche](/wiki/Steve_Larouche "Steve Larouche") and [Jeff Madill](/wiki/Jeff_Madill "Jeff Madill") as well as the blueline brilliance of [Jeff Buchanan](/wiki/Jeff_Buchanan "Jeff Buchanan"), [Eric Charron](/wiki/Eric_Charron "Eric Charron"), [Cory Cross](/wiki/Cory_Cross "Cory Cross") & [Eric Dubois](/wiki/Eric_Dubois "Eric Dubois") contributed to the Knights' second-consecutive division championship.

The 1993–94 season also saw Knights' legend [Stan Drulia](/wiki/Stan_Drulia "Stan Drulia") establish single-season club records for goals (54), assists (60), and points (114). The captain's extraordinary play carried over into the post season, culminating in his being awarded the [Norman R. "Bud" Poile Trophy (IHL)](/wiki/Norman_R._%22Bud%22_Poile_Trophy_(IHL) "Norman R. \"Bud\" Poile Trophy (IHL)") as MVP of the Turner Cup Playoffs with a 13-goal, 25 point performance over the tournament's 14-game span.

4-0 series sweeps in each of the Turner Cup Playoff's first two rounds (vs. the [Milwaukee Admirals](/wiki/Milwaukee_Admirals "Milwaukee Admirals") and [San Diego Gulls (1990–95)](/wiki/San_Diego_Gulls_(1990%E2%80%9395) "San Diego Gulls (1990–95)") respectively) earned the Knights their first Finals appearance. Greenlay's brilliant play continued throughout the tournament with the netminder posting an eye-popping 11–1 record, a microscopic 2.32 GAA & an outstanding .910 save percentage in 13 playoff appearances.

#### 1994–95: The Knightmare Continues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlanta_Knights&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1994–95: The Knightmare Continues")]

#### 1995–96: Waking From the Knightmare – Relocation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Atlanta_Knights&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1995–96: Waking From the Knightmare – Relocation")]

For the 1996–97 season, the Knights relocated due to the Omni's impending demolition to make way for [Philips Arena](/wiki/Philips_Arena "Philips Arena"), a facility that would become home to Atlanta's second [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") expansion team, the [Atlanta Thrashers](/wiki/Atlanta_Thrashers "Atlanta Thrashers"). During their years in Atlanta, Rick Morgan was their full-time national anthem singer and encouraged the crowd to shout "KNIGHTS!" during the line, "Gave proof through the "KNIGHTS" that our flag was still there."[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was customary to hear an emphasized, shouted "KNIGHTS!" during the [national anthem](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner") at [Atlanta Thrashers](/wiki/Atlanta_Thrashers "Atlanta Thrashers") games at [Philips Arena](/wiki/Philips_Arena "Philips Arena") by spectators in honor of their team, and the tradition has carried over to [Atlanta United FC](/wiki/Atlanta_United_FC "Atlanta United FC") soccer matches at [Mercedes-Benz Stadium](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_Stadium "Mercedes-Benz Stadium") and to [Atlanta Gladiators](/wiki/Atlanta_Gladiators "Atlanta Gladiators") games at [Gas South Arena](/wiki/Gas_South_Arena "Gas South Arena").
